elements.name: Agile
elements.desc: A mindset for delivering value through iterative, incremental work, continuous feedback, and responding to change over following a fixed plan. Codified in the 2001 Agile Manifesto.

refs.title: Agile Software Development with Scrum — Schwaber & Beedle
A Brief History of the Agile Movement (Atlassian)
The State of Agile — Martin Fowler

elements.name: Manifesto
elements.desc: The Agile Manifesto: four values and twelve principles signed by 17 practitioners in 2001. Prioritizes individuals, working software, customer collaboration, and responsiveness to change.

refs.title: History: The Agile Manifesto — Jim Highsmith
Agile Software Development: The Cooperative Game — Alistair Cockburn

elements.name: Scrum
elements.desc: A lightweight framework for delivering complex products via short, time-boxed Sprints. Defines three accountabilities, three artifacts, and five events.

refs.title: Scrum: The Art of Doing Twice the Work in Half the Time — Jeff Sutherland

elements.name: Kanban
elements.desc: A flow-based method that visualizes work, limits work in progress (WIP), and improves continuously. Originated from Toyota and adapted to knowledge work by David Anderson.

elements.name: Empiricism
elements.desc: The pillar of Scrum: knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what is observed. Built on transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

refs.title: Empiricism: The Foundation of Scrum — Scrum.org

elements.name: Transparency
elements.desc: Making work, progress, and impediments visible to everyone involved so decisions can be based on shared, accurate information.

elements.name: Inspection
elements.desc: Frequently examining artifacts and progress toward goals to detect undesirable variances or problems early.

elements.name: Adaptation
elements.desc: Adjusting process or product as soon as inspection reveals deviation. Makes change cheap rather than catastrophic.

refs.title: Scrum: The Art of Doing Twice the Work — Sutherland

elements.name: Value
elements.desc: Anything the customer is willing to pay for. In Lean, everything else is waste and a candidate for elimination.

refs.title: The Five Principles of Lean — Lean Enterprise Institute

elements.name: Waste
elements.desc: Muda: any activity that consumes resources without creating customer value. Lean identifies seven (or eight) classic forms including overproduction, waiting, and defects.

refs.title: Lean Software Development — Mary & Tom Poppendieck
The 7 Wastes of Lean — Lean Enterprise Institute

elements.name: Product owner
elements.desc: The Scrum accountability for maximizing product value. Owns the Product Backlog, decides priority, and is the single voice of stakeholders to the team.

refs.title: What is a Product Owner? — Scrum.org
The Professional Product Owner — Don McGreal & Ralph Jocham

elements.name: Scrum master
elements.desc: A servant-leader accountable for the team's effectiveness. Coaches, removes impediments, and ensures Scrum is understood and enacted.

refs.title: What is a Scrum Master? — Scrum.org

elements.name: Developers
elements.desc: The cross-functional people who create the Increment each Sprint. Self-managing and collectively accountable for the Sprint Goal.

refs.title: Scrum: A Pocket Guide — Gunther Verheyen

elements.name: Sprint
elements.desc: A fixed time-box of one month or less during which a usable, valuable Increment is produced. The container event for all other Scrum events.

refs.title: What is a Sprint in Scrum? — Atlassian
Scrum: A Pocket Guide — Gunther Verheyen

elements.name: Planning
elements.desc: The Sprint event where the team forecasts what to deliver, defines the Sprint Goal, and creates the Sprint Backlog.

refs.title: Agile Estimating and Planning — Mike Cohn

elements.name: Daily scrum
elements.desc: A 15-minute event for Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the plan for the next 24 hours.

refs.title: It's Not Just Standing Up — Jason Yip

elements.name: Review
elements.desc: The Sprint event where the team and stakeholders inspect the Increment, gather feedback, and adapt the Product Backlog.

refs.title: Sprint Reviews are Not Demos — Scrum.org

elements.name: Retrospective
elements.desc: The Sprint-closing event where the team inspects how it worked and identifies improvements to enact next Sprint.

refs.title: Agile Retrospectives — Esther Derby & Diana Larsen

elements.name: Product backlog
elements.desc: An ordered, ever-evolving list of everything that might be needed in the product. The single source of work for the Scrum Team.

refs.title: Agile Product Management with Scrum — Roman Pichler
What is a Product Backlog? — Scrum.org

elements.name: Sprint backlog
elements.desc: The Sprint Goal plus the selected Product Backlog items and a plan for delivering them. Owned and updated by the Developers throughout the Sprint.

refs.title: What is a Sprint Backlog? — Scrum.org
Sprint Backlog vs Product Backlog — Atlassian

elements.name: Increment
elements.desc: A concrete, usable step toward the Product Goal. Each Increment must meet the Definition of Done and is additive to all prior Increments.

elements.name: Done
elements.desc: Definition of Done: the shared agreement describing the quality bar a Product Backlog item must meet to be considered part of the Increment.

refs.title: Walking Through the Definition of Done — Mike Cohn
Definition of Done Examples — Scrum Inc

elements.name: Acceptance
elements.desc: Acceptance criteria: item-level conditions of satisfaction. Define what "working as expected" looks like for one Product Backlog item, distinct from the Definition of Done.

refs.title: Acceptance Criteria for User Stories — Mike Cohn

elements.name: User story
elements.desc: A short, customer-centric description of a need, often in the form: "As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit]." A placeholder for a conversation.

refs.title: The 3 Cs of User Stories — Ron Jeffries
INVEST in Good Stories — Bill Wake

elements.name: Epic
elements.desc: A large body of work that can be broken down into multiple user stories, usually delivering a significant chunk of customer value.

refs.title: Epics, Stories, Themes, and Initiatives — Atlassian
Epics vs Features vs Stories — Scaled Agile

elements.name: Theme
elements.desc: A grouping of related epics aligned to a strategic objective or capability area.

refs.title: Themes vs Epics vs Stories — Atlassian
Agile Estimating and Planning — Mike Cohn

elements.name: Sprint goal
elements.desc: The single objective for a Sprint that gives the team coherence and a reason to work together. Lifts work above a checklist of items.

refs.title: The Power of the Sprint Goal — Scrum.org (Robert Pieper)

elements.name: Product goal
elements.desc: A long-term objective that the Scrum Team plans against. The Product Backlog emerges to fulfill it.

elements.name: WIP limit
elements.desc: A cap on how many items can be in a workflow stage at once. Forces focus, exposes bottlenecks, and is the single most important Kanban practice.

refs.title: Stop Starting, Start Finishing — Arne Roock

elements.name: Board
elements.desc: The visual representation of the workflow, with columns for each stage and cards for work items. Makes flow and bottlenecks visible to all.

refs.title: Anatomy of a Kanban Board — Kanbanize
Kanban from the Inside — Mike Burrows

elements.name: Cadence
elements.desc: A regular rhythm for events such as replenishment, delivery, and review. Kanban favors decoupled cadences chosen to fit the work.

refs.title: Kanban from the Inside — Mike Burrows

elements.name: Pull system
elements.desc: Work is pulled into the next stage only when there is capacity, rather than being pushed by an upstream schedule. Smooths flow and prevents overload.

elements.name: Flow
elements.desc: The smooth, uninterrupted movement of value from idea to customer. Improving flow means reducing delays, batches, and handoffs.

refs.title: The Principles of Product Development Flow — Don Reinertsen

elements.name: Bottleneck
elements.desc: The slowest step in a workflow that constrains the throughput of the entire system. Made visible by WIP limits and queues.

elements.name: Cycle time
elements.desc: Elapsed time from when work starts on an item to when it is done. A lagging indicator of how responsive the system is.

refs.title: Actionable Agile Metrics for Predictability — Daniel Vacanti
Cycle Time vs Lead Time — Atlassian

elements.name: Lead time
elements.desc: Elapsed time from when an item is requested to when it is delivered. Measures customer experience of responsiveness.

refs.title: Lead Time vs Cycle Time — Atlassian

elements.name: Throughput
elements.desc: The number of items completed per unit of time. With WIP and lead time it forms Little's Law: WIP = Throughput × Lead time.

refs.title: When Will It Be Done? — Daniel Vacanti

elements.name: Velocity
elements.desc: A team's historical average of completed work (often in story points) per Sprint. A planning aid, not a performance target.

refs.title: Velocity is Killing Agility — Jim Highsmith
Velocity is not the Goal — Scrum.org

elements.name: Burndown
elements.desc: A chart tracking remaining work over time within a Sprint or release. Visualizes whether the team is on track to finish.

elements.name: Burnup
elements.desc: A chart showing work completed against total scope over time. Makes scope changes visible, unlike a burndown.

refs.title: Why I Prefer Burnup Charts — Mike Cohn

elements.name: CFD
elements.desc: Cumulative Flow Diagram: stacked area chart showing the count of items in each workflow state over time. Reveals WIP, lead time, and bottlenecks at a glance.

refs.title: How to Read a CFD — Daniel Vacanti

elements.name: Story map
elements.desc: A two-dimensional arrangement of user stories along the user's journey, sliced into releases. Created by Jeff Patton.

refs.title: The New User Story Backlog is a Map — Jeff Patton

elements.name: MVP
elements.desc: Minimum Viable Product: the smallest version of a product that lets you start the build-measure-learn loop with real users. From Eric Ries' Lean Startup.

refs.title: Minimum Viable Product: a guide — Eric Ries
Making Sense of MVP — Henrik Kniberg

elements.name: MLP
elements.desc: Minimum Lovable Product: an MVP raised to the bar of being delightful to use, not merely sufficient.

refs.title: Why MLP Beats MVP — Brian de Haaff

elements.name: Hypothesis
elements.desc: An explicit, testable assumption about users, value, or feasibility. Lean Startup treats every product idea as a hypothesis until validated.

elements.name: Validated
elements.desc: Validated learning: empirical evidence about what customers actually want, gained by running experiments rather than asking opinions.

elements.name: Pair prog.
elements.desc: Two developers working at one workstation: one drives, one navigates. An XP practice that improves quality, knowledge sharing, and design.

elements.name: Mob prog.
elements.desc: The whole team working together on the same problem at the same computer. Extreme version of pairing for tough problems and learning.

elements.name: TDD
elements.desc: Test-Driven Development: write a failing test, write the minimum code to pass, then refactor. Red-Green-Refactor.

refs.title: Test-Driven Development By Example — Kent Beck
Is TDD Dead? — Martin Fowler / Kent Beck / DHH
Growing Object-Oriented Software, Guided by Tests — Freeman & Pryce

elements.name: BDD
elements.desc: Behavior-Driven Development: TDD framed in business language using Given-When-Then scenarios. Bridges product and engineering.

elements.name: Refactor
elements.desc: Improving the internal structure of code without changing its external behavior. Keeps the cost of change low over time.

refs.title: Working Effectively with Legacy Code — Michael Feathers

elements.name: CI
elements.desc: Continuous Integration: developers merge to a shared mainline frequently, each merge verified by an automated build and tests.

elements.name: CD
elements.desc: Continuous Delivery (or Deployment): every change that passes the pipeline is releasable to production at any time, often automatically.

refs.title: Continuous Delivery — Jez Humble & Dave Farley

elements.name: Tech debt
elements.desc: The accumulated cost of expedient design choices that now slow down change. Must be tracked, paid down, and prevented.

elements.name: Refinement
elements.desc: Backlog refinement: the ongoing activity of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items so they are ready for a future Sprint.

refs.title: Three Levels of Refinement — Roman Pichler

elements.name: Estimation
elements.desc: Forecasting effort or size of work, often relative rather than absolute. Common units include story points and t-shirt sizes.

refs.title: Agile Estimating and Planning — Mike Cohn

elements.name: Poker
elements.desc: Planning Poker: a consensus-based, gamified estimation technique using Fibonacci-like cards revealed simultaneously.

elements.name: Story point
elements.desc: A unit of relative size that bundles complexity, effort, and uncertainty. Compared to a reference story rather than to hours.

refs.title: What Are Story Points? — Mike Cohn

elements.name: Time-box
elements.desc: A fixed maximum duration for an event or activity. Forces decisions, prevents drift, and enables empirical inspection.

elements.name: Workshop
elements.desc: A facilitated working session with explicit purpose, outcomes, and structure. Common formats include kickoffs, retros, and design sprints.

elements.name: Facilitation
elements.desc: The discipline of designing and guiding group interactions toward a clear outcome while staying neutral on content.

refs.title: Training from the Back of the Room — Sharon Bowman

elements.name: Coaching
elements.desc: A partnership in which the coach helps individuals or teams unlock their own thinking and capability. Distinct from mentoring or training.

elements.name: Servant lead.
elements.desc: A leader whose first priority is to serve the team and the work. Originates from Robert Greenleaf and is central to Scrum Master and Agile coaching roles.

refs.title: The Servant as Leader — Robert K. Greenleaf
The Greenleaf Center for Servant Leadership

elements.name: Mentor
elements.desc: A more experienced practitioner who shares knowledge, advice, and perspective with someone earlier in their journey.

elements.name: SAFe
elements.desc: Scaled Agile Framework: a prescriptive framework for applying Lean-Agile across large enterprises with multiple teams, programs, and portfolios.

refs.title: SAFe: A Critical View — Ron Jeffries

elements.name: LeSS
elements.desc: Large-Scale Scrum: an extension of Scrum to multiple teams working on one product, keeping the framework minimal and the product unified.

refs.title: Practices for Scaling Lean & Agile — Larman & Vodde

elements.name: Nexus
elements.desc: A Scrum.org framework that connects 3–9 Scrum Teams working on a single product, adding a Nexus Integration Team and integration events.

refs.title: The Nexus Framework for Scaling Scrum — Bittner et al.

elements.name: Scrum@Scale
elements.desc: Jeff Sutherland's scaling framework that extends Scrum via a Scrum of Scrums and an Executive Action Team, organized as a fractal.

elements.name: Spotify model
elements.desc: An organizational design described by Spotify featuring squads, tribes, chapters, and guilds. Often misapplied as a copy-paste blueprint.

refs.title: Don't Copy the Spotify Model — Jeremiah Lee

elements.name: Squad
elements.desc: A small, cross-functional, autonomous team aligned to a clear mission. The basic unit in the Spotify model.

refs.title: Don't Copy the Spotify Model — Jeremiah Lee

elements.name: Tribe
elements.desc: A collection of squads working in related areas, providing alignment and shared services in the Spotify model.

refs.title: Don't Copy the Spotify Model — Jeremiah Lee

elements.name: Chapter
elements.desc: A horizontal grouping of people with the same specialty across squads, supporting craft growth and consistency.

elements.name: Guild
elements.desc: A voluntary community of interest that crosses tribes and chapters, sharing knowledge on a topic.

elements.name: PI
elements.desc: Program Increment: in SAFe, a fixed cadence (typically 8–12 weeks) during which an Agile Release Train delivers value through aligned Sprints.

elements.name: ART
elements.desc: Agile Release Train: a long-lived team of Agile teams (50–125 people) that delivers value on a common cadence in SAFe.

elements.name: Kaizen
elements.desc: Continuous, incremental improvement carried out by everyone, every day. A cornerstone of the Toyota Production System.

elements.name: Kaikaku
elements.desc: Radical, transformational change, in contrast to Kaizen's small steps. Used when incremental improvement is insufficient.

elements.name: Hansei
elements.desc: Honest self-reflection, especially on what could have gone better, even when results were good. A precondition for genuine Kaizen.

refs.title: Hansei: The Heart of Kaizen — Toyota

elements.name: Genchi
elements.desc: Genchi Genbutsu: "go and see for yourself." Make decisions based on direct observation of the actual place and work, not reports.

elements.name: Gemba
elements.desc: A structured visit to where the work is done so leaders can observe, ask, and respect rather than direct from a distance.

elements.name: Poka-yoke
elements.desc: Mistake-proofing: design choices that make errors impossible or immediately obvious. E.g., USB-C, paired field validation.

refs.title: Zero Quality Control: Poka-Yoke System — Shigeo Shingo

elements.name: Andon
elements.desc: A signal (originally a cord on the Toyota line) that anyone can pull to stop work and surface a problem immediately.

elements.name: JIT
elements.desc: Just-in-Time: producing or delivering only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed. Reduces inventory and exposes problems.

elements.name: Jidoka
elements.desc: Automation with a human touch: machines and people stop work the moment a defect is detected, preventing it from flowing downstream.

elements.name: VSM
elements.desc: Value Stream Mapping: a visual technique that maps every step from request to delivery, distinguishing value-add from waste and exposing delays.

elements.name: 5S
elements.desc: A workplace organization method: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. Creates the conditions for stable, visible work.

elements.name: 3M
elements.desc: Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden): the three enemies of flow that Lean seeks to eliminate.

refs.title: The Three Ms — Lean Enterprise Institute

elements.name: Heijunka
elements.desc: Production leveling: smoothing the type and quantity of work over time to reduce Mura and stabilize flow.

elements.name: Takt time
elements.desc: Customer demand rhythm: available time divided by customer demand. Sets the pace at which the system must deliver.

elements.name: A3
elements.desc: A structured one-page problem-solving and storytelling format on A3 paper, used in Toyota for thinking, alignment, and learning.

elements.name: 5 Whys
elements.desc: A simple root-cause analysis technique: keep asking "why?" (typically five times) until you reach a cause worth addressing.

elements.name: PDCA
elements.desc: Plan-Do-Check-Act: Deming's cycle of continuous improvement. The empirical engine behind Kaizen and Scrum.

refs.title: Out of the Crisis — W. Edwards Deming

elements.name: OKR
elements.desc: Objectives and Key Results: a goal-setting framework pairing a qualitative objective with measurable key results. Popularized by Andy Grove and John Doerr.

elements.name: Impact map
elements.desc: A planning technique linking goals → actors → impacts → deliverables, helping teams choose the right work to reach an outcome.

refs.title: Impact Mapping in Practice — Gojko Adzic

elements.name: Empathy map
elements.desc: A canvas capturing what a user says, thinks, does, and feels. Builds shared understanding before designing solutions.

refs.title: Updated Empathy Map Canvas — Dave Gray

elements.name: Persona
elements.desc: A fictional but evidence-based character representing a user segment. Helps teams design for specific people, not "users" in the abstract.

refs.title: The Inmates Are Running the Asylum — Alan Cooper

elements.name: Def. ready
elements.desc: Definition of Ready: an agreement on the conditions a Product Backlog item should meet before being pulled into a Sprint. Optional but common.

elements.name: Swarming
elements.desc: The team collectively focusing on finishing one item before starting another. A behavior that lowers WIP and increases throughput.

refs.title: Swarming: A Way to Get Things Done — Mike Cohn

elements.name: Spike
elements.desc: A time-boxed investigation to reduce uncertainty about a story, technology, or design. Output is knowledge, not shippable code.

elements.name: Psych. safety
elements.desc: A shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Identified by Amy Edmondson as the strongest predictor of team performance.

refs.title: Building a psychologically safe workplace — Amy Edmondson
